Ross Ice Shelf project : environmental impact statement.

The Ross Ice Shelf Project (RISP) aimed to explore the Ross Ice Shelf in Antarctica by drilling through the ice to study the water and sea floor beneath. Conducted by the University of Nebraska, the project sought to understand the physical, chemical, biological, and geological conditions of the area. The research was expected to provide insights into the Antarctic environment and its global significance. While the project anticipated minimal environmental impact, concerns about potential oil spills and waste management were addressed with preventive measures.

The Ross Ice Shelf Project was proposed to enhance scientific understanding of the Antarctic environment, particularly its physical and biological systems. While the project aimed to minimize environmental impact, there were concerns from environmentalists regarding the risks of drilling, including potential oil spills and waste management issues. Ultimately, the project proceeded with preventive measures in place to address these concerns.
"The possibility of encountering hydrocarbons upon drilling into the sea bottom sediments beneath the ice shelf was of prime importance to those planning the project." — Review Comments and Responses

"It is obviously better to avoid the problem of dealing with an oil slick than it would be to control it after it appeared." — Replies to letter of November 18, 1974, Slator Blackiston, Jr. (State Dept.) to T. O. Jones (NSF)
